title: Apply: kinetic energy
description: Apply your knowledge of kinetic energy in this set of free practice questions.

assessment_items.question_content:
A person walking outside has a kinetic energy of $50\,\text{J}.$

**Which of these objects could also have a kinetic energy of $50\,\text{J}?$**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: If two objects move at the same speed, the more massive one has more kinetic energy. If two moving objects have the same mass, the one moving faster has more kinetic energy.
So, for an object to have the same $50\,\text{J}$ of kinetic energy as the person walking, it could have more mass and a slower speed, or less mass and a faster speed.

A car is more massive than a person. So for it to have the same $50\,\text{J}$ of kinetic energy, it must have a slower speed.

A car driving past the person has a faster speed than the person walking. So, it can’t have the same kinetic energy as the person.  

A parked car is not moving at all.  An object that isn't moving has zero kinetic energy. So, the parked car can’t have $50\,\text{J}$ of kinetic energy.

A baseball is less massive than a person. So for it to have the same $50\,\text{J}$ of kinetic energy, it must have a faster speed.

A baseball in the person’s pocket would move at the same speed as the person. So, it would have less kinetic energy than the person due to its smaller mass. 

But a baseball just after being thrown by the person would have a faster speed than the person. So even though it has less mass, it could have the same kinetic energy as the person because it moves at a faster speed.

The answer is:

a baseball thrown by the person, just after leaving their hand

choices.content: a baseball in the person's pocket
a baseball thrown by the person, just after leaving their hand
a car driving past the person
a car parked on the street

assessment_items.question_content:
A small dog has a kinetic energy of $20\,\text{J}$ while walking.

**Which of these objects could also have a kinetic energy of $20\,\text{J}?$**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: If two objects move at the same speed, the more massive one has more kinetic energy. If two moving objects have the same mass, the one moving faster has more kinetic energy.
So, for an object to have the same $20\,\text{J}$ of kinetic energy as the walking dog, it must have more mass and a slower speed, or less mass and a faster speed.

A man is more massive than a small dog. So for the man to have the same $20\,\text{J}$ of kinetic energy, he must have a slower speed.

A man walking alongside the dog is moving at the same speed as the dog. So, he can’t have the same kinetic energy as the dog.  

A man standing still is not moving at all. An object that isn't moving has zero kinetic energy. So, the man standing still can’t have $20\,\text{J}$ of kinetic energy.

A frisbee is less massive than a dog. So, in order for it to have the same $20\,\text{J}$ of kinetic energy, it must have a faster speed.

A frisbee in the dog's mouth would move at the same speed as the dog. So, it would have less kinetic energy than the dog due to its smaller mass. 

But a frisbee that's just been thrown for the dog to fetch would have a faster speed than the dog. So even though it has less mass, it could have the same kinetic energy as the dog because it moves at a faster speed.

The answer is:

a frisbee that's just been thrown for the dog to fetch

choices.content: a man standing still watching the dog
a man walking alongside the dog
a frisbee the dog is carrying in her mouth
a frisbee that's just been thrown for the dog to fetch

assessment_items.question_content:
A small car moving at a constant speed on a city street has a kinetic energy of $200{,}000\,\text{J}.$

**Which of these objects could also have a kinetic energy of $200{,}000\,\text{J}?$**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: If two objects move at the same speed, the more massive one has more kinetic energy. If two moving objects have the same mass, the one moving faster has more kinetic energy.
So, for an object to have the same $200{,}000\,\text{J}$ of kinetic energy as the car, it must have more mass and a slower speed, or less mass and a faster speed.

A person is less massive than a car. So for a person to have the same $200{,}000\,\text{J}$ of kinetic energy, they must have a faster speed.

The walking person is passed by the car, so we know they're moving at a slower speed than the car. They can’t have the same kinetic energy as the car.  

The person driving the car is moving at the same speed as the car. So, they can’t have the same kinetic energy as the car.

A semi-truck is more massive than a car. So, in order for it to have the same $200{,}000\,\text{J}$ of kinetic energy, it must have a smaller speed.

A semi-truck moving at the same speed as the car, therefore, can't have the same kinetic energy as the car.

But a semi-truck starting to speed up after a red light turns green would have a smaller speed than the car. So even though it has more mass, it could have the same kinetic energy as the car because it moves at a smaller speed.

The answer is:

a semi-truck starting to speed up after a red light turns green

choices.content: a person walking on the sidewalk who gets passed by the car
a semi-truck starting to speed up after a red light turns green
a semi-truck moving at the same speed as the car

assessment_items.question_content:
A father and daughter are riding their bikes at the same speed. The father's mass is three times the daughter's mass.

[[☃ image 1]]

**If the father’s kinetic energy is $300\,\text{J},$ what is the daughter's kinetic energy?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] $\text{J}$
hints: An object’s kinetic energy depends on its mass and speed. The father and daughter move at the same speed. So, we can compare their masses to compare their kinetic energies.

For objects moving at the same speed, there is a **linear relationship** between mass and kinetic energy. This means that mass and kinetic energy follow the same pattern—if one object's mass is larger or smaller by some factor, its kinetic energy is larger or smaller by that *same factor*.

The father's mass is three times the daughter's mass. Since they move at the same speed, this means the father's kinetic energy must be three times daughter's kinetic energy.

Or, we can say the daughter's mass is one-third the father's mass. So, the daughter's kinetic energy must be one-third the father's kinetic energy.

We're told the father has $300\,\text{J}$ of kinetic energy. So, the daughter's kinetic energy must be one-third of $300\,\text{J}:$

$\dfrac{1}{3}\times300\,\text{J}=100\,\text{J}$
The answer is $\boxed{100\,\text{J}}.$

options.alt: A side view of a man and child riding bicycles next to each other.

assessment_items.question_content:
A refueling aircraft transfers fuel to a fighter jet flying at the same speed. The refueling aircraft's mass is ten times the fighter jet's mass.

[[☃ image 2]]

**If the fighter jet's kinetic energy is $0.15\,\text{GJ}$ ([[☃ definition 1]]), what is the refueling aircraft's kinetic energy?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] $\text{GJ}$
hints: An object’s kinetic energy depends on its mass and speed. The refueling aircraft and fighter jet move at the same speed. So, we can compare their masses to compare their kinetic energies.

For objects moving at the same speed, there is a **linear relationship** between mass and kinetic energy. This means that mass and kinetic energy follow the same pattern—if one object's mass is larger or smaller by some factor, its kinetic energy is larger or smaller by that *same factor*.

The refueling aircraft's mass is ten times the fighter jet's mass. Since they move at the same speed, this means the refueling aircraft's kinetic energy must be ten times the fighter jet's kinetic energy.

We're told the fighter jet has $0.15\,\text{GJ}$ of kinetic energy. So, the refueling aircraft's kinetic energy must be ten times $0.15\,\text{GJ}:$

$10\times0.15\,\text{GJ}=1.5\,\text{GJ}$
The answer is $\boxed{1.5\,\text{GJ}}.$

options.definition: One gigajoule equals one billion joules.

options.alt: A large plane and a smaller jet, connected by a long thin rod and hose, are silhouetted against the sky above clouds.

assessment_items.question_content:
A mother throws a bowling ball, giving it $180\,\text{J}$ of kinetic energy.

[[☃ image 2]]

Then, her son throws his bowling ball at the same speed. His ball has one-half the mass of the mother's ball. 

**How much kinetic energy did the son give his bowling ball?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] $\text{J}$
hints: An object’s kinetic energy depends on its mass and speed. The mother's and son's bowling balls move at the same speed. So, we can compare their masses to compare their kinetic energies.

For objects moving at the same speed, there is a **linear relationship** between mass and kinetic energy. This means that mass and kinetic energy follow the same pattern—if one object's mass is larger or smaller by some factor, its kinetic energy is larger or smaller by that *same factor*.
The son's ball is half the mass of the mother's ball. Since they move at the same speed, this means the kinetic energy of the son's ball must be half that of the mother's ball.

We're told the mother's ball has $180\,\text{J}$ of kinetic energy. So, the kinetic energy of the son's ball must be one-half of $180\,\text{J}:$

$\dfrac{1}{2}\times180\,\text{J}=90\,\text{J}$
The answer is $\boxed{90\,\text{J}}.$

options.alt: A woman rolls a bowling ball while standing next to a small boy who is holding another bowling ball.

assessment_items.question_content:
A roller coaster gains speed as it moves downhill. 

[[☃ image 4]]

A graph relating the coaster’s kinetic energy in [[☃ definition 1]] $(\text{MJ})$ and its speed in meters per second $\left(\tfrac{\text{m}}{\text{s}}\right)$ is shown below.

[[☃ image 2]]

**Use the pattern on the graph to complete the prediction.**

* When the coaster’s speed is $40\,\tfrac{\text{m}}{\text{s}},$ its kinetic energy will be [[☃ numeric-input 1]] $\text{MJ}.$
hints: We can determine how kinetic energy is related to speed using values from the graph. Then, we can use that relationship to predict the coaster’s kinetic energy when its speed is $40\,\tfrac{\text{m}}{\text{s}},$ which is *not* shown on the graph.

Let’s see how the coaster’s kinetic energy changes when its speed doubles from $10\,\tfrac{\text{m}}{\text{s}}$ to $20\,\tfrac{\text{m}}{\text{s}}.$

[[☃ image 1]]

When the coaster’s speed is $\color{#0000ff}10\,\tfrac{\text{m}}{\text{s}}\color{#000000},$ its kinetic energy is $\color{#0000ff}0.5\,\text{MJ}\color{#000000}.$

When the coaster’s speed is doubled to $\color{#ff0000}20\,\tfrac{\text{m}}{\text{s}}\color{#000000},$ its kinetic energy is $\color{#ff0000}2.0\,\text{MJ}\color{#000000}.$

$\color{#ff0000}2.0\,\text{MJ}$ is *four times* as much as $\color{#0000ff}0.5\,\text{MJ}$ $\left(\dfrac{\color{#ff0000}2.0\,\text{MJ}}{\color{#0000ff}0.5\,\text{MJ}}=4\right).$

When the speed was doubled (multiplied by a factor of $2$), the kinetic energy was multiplied by a factor of $2$ *twice:* 

$\color{#0000ff}0.5\,\text{MJ}\color{#000000}\times2\times2=\color{#ff0000}2.0\,\text{MJ}$

When a factor is multiplied by itself we say it is **squared** $(2\times2=2^2=4).$ 

So, the graph shows that kinetic energy depends on speed squared. When the speed is multiplied by some factor, the kinetic energy is multiplied by that same factor *squared.*

We need the coaster’s kinetic energy when its speed is $40\,\tfrac{\text{m}}{\text{s}},$ which is *two times* as much as $20\,\tfrac{\text{m}}{\text{s}}.$  

We determined from the graph that if the speed is multiplied by $2,$ kinetic energy is multiplied by $2$ *squared*, which is $4.$

When the speed is $20\,\tfrac{\text{m}}{\text{s}}$ the coaster’s kinetic energy is $2.0\,\text{MJ}.$  So, when its speed doubles to $40\,\tfrac{\text{m}}{\text{s}}$ its kinetic energy will be *four times* as much:

$2.0\,\text{MJ}\times4=8.0\,\text{MJ}$
The answer is $\boxed{8.0\,\text{MJ}}.$

options.definition: One megajoule equals one million joules.

options.alt:
A graph with kinetic energy in megajoules on the y-axis and speed in meters per second on the x-axis. Starting from the bottom of the y-axis, the increments are labeled 0, 0.5, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0. Starting from the left end of the x-axis, the increments are labeled 0, 5, 10, 15, 20. 
A curved line starts at point (0,0) and curves up and to the right, ending at point (20, 2.0). The shape of the curve looks like the right side of a bowl.
A roller coaster hill with a train full of people beginning to move down the hill.

assessment_items.question_content:
A group of skydivers gains speed as they fall together.

[[☃ image 5]]

A graph relating the group's kinetic energy in [[☃ definition 1]] $(\text{kJ})$ and their speed in meters per second $\left(\tfrac{\text{m}}{\text{s}}\right)$ is shown below.

[[☃ image 3]]

**Use the pattern on the graph to complete the prediction.**

* When the group’s speed is $40\,\tfrac{\text{m}}{\text{s}},$ their kinetic energy will be [[☃ numeric-input 1]] $\text{kJ}.$
hints: We can determine how kinetic energy is related to speed using values from the graph. Then, we can use that relationship to predict the group's kinetic energy when their speed is $40\,\tfrac{\text{m}}{\text{s}},$ which is *not* shown on the graph.

Let’s see how the group's kinetic energy changes when their speed doubles from $10\,\tfrac{\text{m}}{\text{s}}$ to $20\,\tfrac{\text{m}}{\text{s}}.$

[[☃ image 2]]

When the group's speed is $\color{#0000ff}10\,\tfrac{\text{m}}{\text{s}}\color{#000000},$ their kinetic energy is $\color{#0000ff}20\,\text{kJ}\color{#000000}.$

When the group's speed is doubled to $\color{#ff0000}20\,\tfrac{\text{m}}{\text{s}}\color{#000000},$ their kinetic energy is $\color{#ff0000}80\,\text{kJ}\color{#000000}.$

$\color{#ff0000}80\,\text{kJ}$ is *four times* as much as $\color{#0000ff}20\,\text{kJ}$ $\left(\dfrac{\color{#ff0000}80\,\text{kJ}}{\color{#0000ff}20\,\text{kJ}}=4\right).$

When the speed was doubled (multiplied by a factor of $2$), the kinetic energy was multiplied by a factor of $2$ *twice:* 

$\color{#0000ff}20\,\text{kJ}\color{#000000}\times2\times2=\color{#ff0000}80\,\text{kJ}$

When a factor is multiplied by itself we say it is **squared** $(2\times2=2^2=4).$ 

So, the graph shows that kinetic energy depends on speed squared. When the speed is multiplied by some factor, the kinetic energy is multiplied by that same factor *squared.*

We need the group's kinetic energy when their speed is $40\,\tfrac{\text{m}}{\text{s}},$ which is *two times* as much as $20\,\tfrac{\text{m}}{\text{s}}.$  

We determined from the graph that if the speed is multiplied by $2,$ kinetic energy is multiplied by $2$ *squared*, which is $4.$

When the speed is $20\,\tfrac{\text{m}}{\text{s}}$ the group's kinetic energy is $80\,\text{kJ}.$  So, when their speed doubles to $40\,\tfrac{\text{m}}{\text{s}}$ their kinetic energy will be *four times* as much:

$80\,\text{kJ}\times4=320\,\text{kJ}$
The answer is $\boxed{320\,\text{kJ}}.$

options.definition: One kilojoule equals one thousand joules.

options.alt:
A graph with kinetic energy in kilojoules on the y-axis and speed in meters per second on the x-axis. Starting from the bottom of the y-axis, the increments are labeled 0, 20, 40, 60, 80. Starting from the left end of the x-axis, the increments are labeled 0, 5, 10, 15, 20. 
A curved line starts at point (0,0) and curves up and to the right, ending at point (20,80). The shape of the curve looks like the right side of a bowl.
Six people hold hands to form a ring while falling.

assessment_items.question_content:
The average housecat can run faster than a human.  

[[☃ image 5]]

A graph relating a cat's kinetic energy in joules $(\text{J})$ and its speed in meters per second $\left(\tfrac{\text{m}}{\text{s}}\right)$ is shown below.

[[☃ image 4]]

**Use the pattern on the graph to complete the prediction.**

* When the cat’s speed is $12\,\tfrac{\text{m}}{\text{s}},$ its kinetic energy will be [[☃ numeric-input 1]] $\text{J}.$
hints: We can determine how kinetic energy is related to speed using values from the graph. Then, we can use that relationship to predict the cat’s kinetic energy when its speed is $12\,\tfrac{\text{m}}{\text{s}},$ which is *not* shown on the graph.

Let’s see how the cat’s kinetic energy changes when its speed doubles from $3\,\tfrac{\text{m}}{\text{s}}$ to $6\,\tfrac{\text{m}}{\text{s}}.$

[[☃ image 2]]

When the cat's speed is $\color{#0000ff}3\,\tfrac{\text{m}}{\text{s}}\color{#000000},$ its kinetic energy is $\color{#0000ff}25\,\text{J}\color{#000000}.$

When the cat’s speed is doubled to $\color{#ff0000}6\,\tfrac{\text{m}}{\text{s}}\color{#000000},$ its kinetic energy is $\color{#ff0000}100\,\text{J}\color{#000000}.$

$\color{#ff0000}100\,\text{J}$ is *four times* as much as $\color{#0000ff}25\,\text{J}$ $\left(\dfrac{\color{#ff0000}100\,\text{J}}{\color{#0000ff}25\,\text{J}}=4\right).$

When the speed was doubled (multiplied by a factor of $2$), the kinetic energy was multiplied by a factor of $2$ *twice:* 

$\color{#0000ff}25\,\text{J}\color{#000000}\times2\times2=\color{#ff0000}100\,\text{J}$

When a factor is multiplied by itself we say it is **squared** $(2\times2=2^2=4).$ 

So, the graph shows that kinetic energy depends on speed squared. When the speed is multiplied by some factor, the kinetic energy is multiplied by that same factor *squared.*

We need the cat’s kinetic energy when its speed is $12\,\tfrac{\text{m}}{\text{s}},$ which is *two times* as much as $6\,\tfrac{\text{m}}{\text{s}}.$  

We determined from the graph that if the speed is multiplied by $2,$ kinetic energy is multiplied by $2$ *squared*, which is $4.$

When the speed is $6\,\tfrac{\text{m}}{\text{s}}$ the cat’s kinetic energy is $100\,\text{J}.$  So, when its speed doubles to $12\,\tfrac{\text{m}}{\text{s}}$ its kinetic energy will be *four times* as much:

$100\,\text{J}\times4=400\,\text{J}$
The answer is $\boxed{400\,\text{J}}.$

options.alt:
A graph with kinetic energy in joules on the y-axis and speed in meters per second on the x-axis. Starting from the bottom of the y-axis, the increments are labeled 0, 25, 50, 75, 100. Starting from the left end of the x-axis, the increments are labeled 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6. 
A curved line starts at point (0,0) and curves up and to the right, ending at point (6,100). The shape of the curve looks like the right side of a bowl.
A head-on view of two housecats running across the grass.

assessment_items.question_content:
The data table below shows kinetic energies for objects with different masses and speeds. 

mass $(\text{kg})$ | speed $\left(\tfrac{\text{m}}{\text{s}}\right)$ | kinetic energy $(\text{J})$
:-: | :-: | :-:
$1$|$2$|$2$
$2$|$1$|$1$
$2$|$2$|$4$
$2$|$3$|$9$
$2$|$4$|$16$
$3$|$2$|$6$
$4$|$2$|$8$

This data can be graphed to model the relationship between kinetic energy and mass. However, only *certain* data from the table should be plotted together on the same graph.

**Determine which four data points should be graphed to model the relationship between kinetic energy and mass. Then, plot those four points on the graph below.**  
*Drag the blue dot to plot points on the graph.*

[[☃ interactive-graph 1]]
hints: An object’s kinetic energy *depends on* both its mass and speed. So, kinetic energy is the **dependent variable** in the model.
We’re modeling the relationship between kinetic energy and *mass.* So, mass is the **independent variable** in the model. We need to see how kinetic energy changes for different masses.

All other factors which affect the dependent variable must be held constant. Kinetic energy depends on mass *and speed*. So, speed needs to be the same for all the points we plot. Otherwise, any change in kinetic energy would not be due to mass alone.

There are four trials with the same speed $\left(2\,\tfrac{\text{m}}{\text{s}}\right).$  So, the masses and kinetic energies for these trials (highlighted below) are the values we should plot.

mass $(\text{kg})$ | speed $\left(\tfrac{\text{m}}{\text{s}}\right)$ | kinetic energy $(\text{J})$
:-: | :-: | :-:
$\colorbox{yellow}1$|$2$|$\colorbox{yellow}2$
$2$|$1$|$1$
$\colorbox{yellow}2$|$2$|$\colorbox{yellow}4$
$2$|$3$|$9$
$2$|$4$|$16$
$\colorbox{yellow}3$|$2$|$\colorbox{yellow}6$
$\colorbox{yellow}4$|$2$|$\colorbox{yellow}8$

Mass is on the horizontal $(x)$ axis of the graph, and kinetic energy is on the vertical $(y)$ axis of the graph. So, to plot these points we should go *over* the mass amount, and then *up* the kinetic energy amount.

When we graph these four points, we see kinetic energy and mass have a **linear relationship**:

[[☃ interactive-graph 1]]

options.fullGraphAriaDescription: Graph with kinetic energy in joules on the vertical axis, and mass in kilograms on the horizontal axis.

assessment_items.question_content:
The data table below shows kinetic energies for objects with different speeds and masses. 

speed $\left(\tfrac{\text{m}}{\text{s}}\right)$ | mass $(\text{kg})$ | kinetic energy $(\text{J})$
:-: | :-: | :-:
$1$|$2$|$1$
$2$|$1$|$2$
$2$|$2$|$4$
$2$|$3$|$6$
$2$|$4$|$8$
$3$|$2$|$9$
$4$|$2$|$16$

This data can be graphed to model the relationship between kinetic energy and speed. However, only *certain* data from the table should be plotted together on the same graph.

**Determine which four data points should be graphed to model the relationship between kinetic energy and speed. Then, plot those four points on the graph.**  
*Drag the blue dot to plot points on the graph.*

[[☃ interactive-graph 1]]
hints: An object’s kinetic energy *depends on* both its mass and speed. So, kinetic energy is the **dependent variable** in the model.
We’re modeling the relationship between kinetic energy and *speed.* So, speed is the **independent variable** in the model. We need to see how kinetic energy changes for different speeds.

All other factors which affect the dependent variable must be held constant. Kinetic energy depends on speed *and mass*. So, mass needs to be the same for all the points we plot. Otherwise, any change in kinetic energy would not be due to speed alone.

There are four trials with the same mass $(2\,\text{kg}).$  So, the speeds and kinetic energies for these trials (highlighted below) are the values we should plot.

speed $\left(\tfrac{\text{m}}{\text{s}}\right)$ | mass $(\text{kg})$ | kinetic energy $(\text{J})$
:-: | :-: | :-:
$\colorbox{yellow}1$|$2$|$\colorbox{yellow}1$
$2$|$1$|$2$
$\colorbox{yellow}2$|$2$|$\colorbox{yellow}4$
$2$|$3$|$6$
$2$|$4$|$8$
$\colorbox{yellow}3$|$2$|$\colorbox{yellow}9$
$\colorbox{yellow}4$|$2$|$\colorbox{yellow}{16}$

Speed is on the horizontal $(x)$ axis of the graph, and kinetic energy is on the vertical $(y)$ axis of the graph. So, to plot these points we should go *over* the speed amount, and then *up* the kinetic energy amount.

When we graph these four points, we see kinetic energy and speed have a **nonlinear relationship**:

[[☃ interactive-graph 2]]

options.fullGraphAriaDescription: Graph with kinetic energy in joules on the vertical axis, and speed in meters per second on the horizontal axis.

**Determine which four data points should be graphed to model the relationship between kinetic energy and mass. Then, plot those four points on the graph.**

We’re modeling the relationship between kinetic energy and *mass.* So, mass is the **independent variable** in the model. We need to see how kinetic energy changes for different masses.

All other factors which affect the dependent variable must be held constant. Kinetic energy depends on mass *and speed*. So, speed needs to be the same for all the points we plot. Otherwise, any change in kinetic energy would not be due to mass alone.
